codeSnapshot.label: lora_qlora.py
codeSnapshot.code:
# LoRA (Hu et al., 2022) + QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023)
import torch
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType, prepare_model_for_kbit_training
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, BitsAndBytesConfig

# ---- LoRA ----
# ΔW = BA where B ∈ R^{d×r}, A ∈ R^{r×k}, rank r ≪ min(d, k)
# h = W₀x + (α/r)·BAx
# Init: A ~ N(0, σ²), B = 0  →  ΔW = 0 at start

lora_config = LoraConfig(
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
    r=16,                  # rank — 4 to 64; lower r = fewer params
    lora_alpha=32,         # α scaling factor; effective lr multiplier = α/r = 2.0
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    target_modules=[       # which weight matrices to adapt
        'q_proj', 'k_proj', 'v_proj', 'o_proj',   # attention
        'gate_proj', 'up_proj', 'down_proj',        # MLP
    ],
    bias='none',           # don't train biases (they're tiny)
)

# Standard LoRA on full-precision model
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    'meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf',
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map='auto',
)
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# → trainable params: 83,886,080 || all params: 6,771,970,048 || trainable%: 1.24

# ---- QLoRA ----
# Base model in 4-bit NF4 (Normal Float 4, optimal for Gaussian weights)
# Adapters (B, A) in BF16
# Double quantization: also quantize the quantization constants

quantization_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,   # computation in BF16
    bnb_4bit_quant_type='nf4',               # NF4 quantization
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,          # double quantization
)

model_4bit = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    'meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf',
    quantization_config=quantization_config,
    device_map='auto',
)
# Prepare model: cast LayerNorm to FP32, enable gradient checkpointing
model_4bit = prepare_model_for_kbit_training(model_4bit)
model_4bit = get_peft_model(model_4bit, lora_config)
# Memory: 7B in NF4 ≈ 3.5 GB (weights) + 1.0 GB (adapters) ≈ 4.5 GB active GPU
# vs 7B in BF16: 14 GB — QLoRA is ~3x more memory efficient

eli3: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is a technique for fine-tuning large language models cheaply. Instead of updating all billions of weights in a model, LoRA adds tiny 'adapter' matrices to specific layers and only trains those. The base model stays frozen — you're only learning a small correction.

Think of it like attaching a thin overlay to a painting instead of repainting the whole canvas. The overlay captures what needs to change, while the original painting stays intact. LoRA adapters for a 7B model can be as small as 50–100MB, making them easy to share and swap.

QLoRA takes this further: it compresses the frozen base model to 4-bit precision (taking 4× less memory), while keeping the adapters in full precision for accurate gradient computation. This makes fine-tuning a 65B model feasible on a single 48GB GPU — something that previously required 8× 80GB GPUs.

whenWhy.title: Fine-tuning large models on limited hardware
whenWhy.body: Full fine-tuning of a 7B LLM in BF16 requires ~120GB GPU memory (model + optimizer states + gradients). LoRA with r=16 trains only ~1% of parameters, reducing optimizer states and gradients proportionally: ~20GB for 7B LoRA. QLoRA further reduces this to ~6GB for 7B (NF4 base + BF16 adapters + paged optimizer), making 7B fine-tuning feasible on a single 8GB consumer GPU. QLoRA enabled the open-source LLM fine-tuning boom (WizardLM, OpenHermes, Orca-2 all used QLoRA).

whenWhy.title: Task-specific adapters without catastrophic forgetting
whenWhy.body: LoRA preserves the original model weights (W₀ never changes) — the adaptation is entirely in ΔW = BA. This means: (1) No catastrophic forgetting of pre-training knowledge; (2) Multiple LoRA adapters can be trained for different tasks and swapped at inference time without reloading the base model (LoRA Hub); (3) The base model + many task adapters pattern is emerging as the standard for multi-task LLM deployment. vLLM and SGLang support LoRA adapter hot-swapping for efficient multi-tenant serving.

whenWhy.title: Nearly matching full fine-tune quality at 1/100th the cost
whenWhy.body: Hu et al. (2022) showed LoRA with r=4 matches full fine-tune on GPT-3 on commonsense reasoning and NLU tasks. Dettmers et al. (2023) showed QLoRA (4-bit base + BF16 adapters) matches full BF16 fine-tune on Vicuna benchmark across model sizes (7B, 13B, 33B, 65B). The quality ceiling: LoRA may underperform full fine-tune on tasks requiring extensive weight changes (e.g., learning a new language), but for instruction tuning and task adaptation it is consistently competitive.

deepDiveIntro: LoRA (Hu et al., 2022) adds a low-rank weight update to frozen base layers. The update is the product of two small matrices — one of size d-by-r and one of size r-by-k — where the rank r is much smaller than the original dimensions. The modified forward pass adds this low-rank correction, scaled by a hyperparameter ratio, to the frozen layer output. The correction matrix starts at zero because one factor is initialized to zero, so training begins from exactly the pre-trained model. Only the two small matrices are updated, reducing trainable parameters to about one percent of the original. [1]

QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023) combines three ideas to enable 65B model fine-tuning on a single 48GB GPU. First, NF4 quantization uses quantization thresholds based on the quantiles of a normal distribution, which is the optimal placement for normally distributed weights. Second, double quantization compresses the quantization constants themselves, saving additional memory. Third, paged optimizers move optimizer states between GPU and CPU memory as needed to avoid out-of-memory errors during gradient computation. [2]
formula: h = W₀x + (α/r)·BAx,  B ∈ R^{d×r},  A ∈ R^{r×k}

deepDive.heading: LoRA Forward Pass and Rank Selection
deepDive.body: The scaling ratio of the hyperparameter to the rank controls how strongly the adapter influences the model relative to the frozen weights. A standard setting is alpha equals twice the rank, giving a scale factor of two. Rank four is suitable for minimal parameter count, eight to sixteen covers most instruction-tuning tasks, and thirty-two to sixty-four is used for complex domain adaptation. Applying adapters to all linear layers including feedforward matrices consistently outperforms applying them only to attention projections, at the cost of roughly twice as many adapter parameters. [1]

deepDive.heading: NF4 Quantization (QLoRA)
deepDive.body: Standard 4-bit quantization places the sixteen representable values at evenly spaced intervals, which wastes resolution in the center of the distribution where most weight values fall. NF4 instead places quantization thresholds at the quantiles of a standard normal distribution, so each bucket contains an equal fraction of probability mass. This is information-theoretically optimal for normally distributed weights and in practice matches INT8 quality at half the bit width, enabling close to lossless four-times compression. [2]

deepDive.heading: Memory Analysis: LoRA vs QLoRA vs Full FT
deepDive.body: Full fine-tuning of a 7B model in BF16 requires roughly 120GB for weights, optimizer states, and gradients. LoRA with a BF16 base reduces this to about 20GB because only the adapter parameters need optimizer states. QLoRA cuts the base weights to NF4 at 3.5GB and adds about 1GB for the adapters, bringing the total below 6GB — enough to fit on a single consumer GPU. For 65B models the same ratios apply, making 48GB the target with QLoRA versus roughly 780GB for full fine-tuning. [2]

deepDive.heading: LoRA Merging and Serving
deepDive.body: After fine-tuning, the adapter can be merged back into the base weight matrix by adding the scaled low-rank product to the frozen weights. The merged model runs at the same speed as the original base model with no added overhead. Alternatively, keeping adapters separate allows hot-swapping at serving time — a single base model loaded in memory can serve many task-specific fine-tunes by routing each request to the appropriate adapter. Production serving frameworks such as vLLM support this pattern, amortizing the base model cost across many fine-tuned variants. [1]

youtubeLabel: LoRA and QLoRA: Efficient LLM Fine-Tuning Explained
takeaways: LoRA: ΔW=BA (rank-r decomposition), only A and B trained — ~1% of original parameters. [1]
h = W₀x + (α/r)·BAx; initialize B=0 so ΔW=0 at start; α=2r is standard. [1]
QLoRA: NF4 4-bit base + BF16 adapters + double quantization → 65B fine-tune on 48GB GPU. [2]
LoRA nearly matches full fine-tune quality at 1/100th parameters for instruction tuning tasks. [1]
Adapters can be merged (zero inference cost) or hot-swapped for multi-task serving. [1]

edgeCases.q: When does LoRA fail to match full fine-tuning?
edgeCases.a: LoRA underperforms full fine-tuning in several scenarios: (1) Large distribution shift: if the target task is very different from pre-training (e.g., fine-tuning an English model for Chinese), the low-rank constraint limits how much the model can change. Full fine-tuning can update any weight freely; LoRA is limited to rank-r changes. (2) Very large rank: at r = min(d,k), LoRA equals full fine-tuning but loses its efficiency. (3) Long sequence tasks requiring attention pattern changes in many layers: LoRA applied only to attention matrices may miss MLP changes needed for long-context tasks. Mitigations: increase r, apply LoRA to all linear layers (including MLP), or use full fine-tuning for the critical layers and LoRA for others.

edgeCases.q: How do you choose rank r and which layers to apply LoRA to?
edgeCases.a: Rank selection: r=8 is a safe default for instruction tuning. r=16–32 for complex tasks (domain-specific fine-tuning, style transfer). r=4 for efficiency-critical deployments (matches full FT on many tasks). r is usually tuned via validation loss. Layer selection: the original LoRA paper (Hu et al.) applied only to attention weights (q, v) and found this sufficient. Empirically: applying to all linear layers (q, k, v, o, gate, up, down) consistently outperforms attention-only LoRA by 1–3 perplexity points. Ablations in Dettmers et al. (2023) show 'all linear layers' is the recommended default for QLoRA. Bias terms are typically excluded (tiny size, minimal impact).

sources.label: Hu et al. (2022) — LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models arXiv:2106.09685
Dettmers et al. (2023) — QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs arXiv:2305.14314
